75	## Blueprint chapters
76	
77	Informal proofs live in `blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`, one file per Lean source file (`Foo/Bar.lean` → `Foo_Bar.tex`). `blueprint/src/content.tex` `\input`s the chapters; keep it updated. Each chapter contains rigorous prose at textbook level — not sketches.
78	
79	**Consolidated chapters.** When the math for several Lean files is most naturally written as one chapter (and the sibling chapters would just be thin pointers), declare the coverage explicitly at the top of the consolidated chapter:
80	
81	```latex
82	% archon:covers RigidityKbar.lean Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean Cotangent/ChartAlgebraS3.lean
83	```
84	
85	(whitespace- or comma-separated, repeatable across lines). The prover-dispatch gate then treats that one chapter as the blueprint for all listed files, and the blueprint doctor lints the declaration (covered file must exist; no file covered by two chapters). Without a `covers:` line the strict 1:1 slug mapping applies.
86	
87	Before assigning a prover, ensure the relevant chapter file exists and contains the content the prover needs. Each declaration block looks like:
88	
89	```latex
90	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
91	  \label{thm:some_label}
92	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
93	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
94	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne.pdf)
95	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
96	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
97	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
98	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
99	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
100	  Informal statement, in the project's notation.
101	\end{theorem}
102	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
103	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
104	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
105	\begin{proof}
106	  \uses{thm:another_result}
107	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Detail enough to formalize.
108	\end{proof}
109	```
110	
111	**Proof sketches must be mathematical, not syntactic.** No Lean tactics.
112	
113	**Citation discipline.** Every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material MUST include:
114	
115	1. A `% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment naming **(a)** the citation pointer — source identifier, section / theorem / definition number, page when available — AND **(b)** the local file under `references/` it was read from. Format: `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>)`. The `(read from …)` parenthetical is mandatory — it documents which local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote on the next line. Name the actual source file you quoted from — the downloaded PDF/TeX (`references/<slug>.pdf`, `references/<slug>.tex`) when one exists, not its pointer `.md` index card (which holds only a citation + contents map, never quotable text).
116	2. A `% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment containing the **verbatim text** of the cited statement. Verbatim means:
117	   - **In the source's original language** (French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's pre-EGA work, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, …). Do NOT translate.
118	   - **Original notation preserved character-by-character**. If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ where the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The visible project-notation restatement happens AFTER the quote, in the prose body.
119	   - **Every word and every symbol preserved**. No paraphrase. No abbreviation. No "obvious" omissions. If a word feels redundant in the source, it still goes in the quote.
120	   - Long quotes are fine — LaTeX comments don't render in the PDF and don't bloat the typeset output.
121	3. A visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line as the first line of the block's prose (renders in the PDF so the human reader sees the citation at a glance without grep).
122	
123	For **proof blocks**: add a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). It contains the **verbatim original-language proof** from the source — same rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:` (original language, original notation, every word). The informal proof body that follows inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
124	
125	When a source proof is so long that verbatim transcription is impractical (e.g., a multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas, give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` of the corresponding source fragment. The blueprint's purpose is verifiable mathematics; one long opaque block defeats that. If even sub-splitting is impractical, escalate to USER_HINTS — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
126	
127	For **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source), the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
128	
129	**The hard rule: NEVER cite a source you have not just read locally.** Writing `% SOURCE:` or `% SOURCE QUOTE:` or `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` or `\textit{Source: …}` from memory is a fabrication, full stop. The `(read from references/<file>)` parenthetical is your discipline check: the named local file must exist, you must have opened and read it this session, and the verbatim quote on the next line must be copied from that file. If you do not have the local file:
130	
131	- Dispatch a literature/reference-fetching subagent from your catalog (it downloads the original source file — PDF and TeX when available — into `references/` and writes a pointer `references/<slug>.md` index card; you then open the downloaded source and quote it verbatim), OR use `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` directly and write the retrieved text to `references/<slug>.md` yourself.
132	- Wait for the file to land.
133	- Open the file and read it.
134	- THEN write the citation block.
135	
136	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key for a tool), leave the block flagged with `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and treat the chapter as gated on retrieval — do not assign provers to formalize an unverified statement. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a "based on my recollection" approximation, or a translation as the verbatim quote.
137	
138	**Markers** are managed deterministically — `\leanok` by the `sync_leanok` phase between prover and review, `\mathlibok` by the review agent. **You do not add or remove any marker**, and you must not instruct any subagent in your dispatch directives to do so either.
139	
140	**LaTeX macros**: define in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` *before* using.
141	
142	In `PROGRESS.md`, next to each objective record which chapter backs it: `**`Foo.lean`** — Blueprint: `chapters/Foo.tex` (theorems `thm:x`, `thm:y`)`.
143	
144	## Long-arc Strategy
145	
146	`STRATEGY.md` is your living arc of how the project gets from the current state to "complete". `PROGRESS.md` scopes the next iteration; `STRATEGY.md` is the arc that contains every iteration. Only you write to it. The mathematician reads it — keep it human-readable.
147	
148	Read it early every iteration. Update it after processing prover/review results, before writing `PROGRESS.md` or the blueprint.
149	
150	### Canonical structure (use this skeleton)
151	
152	`STRATEGY.md` follows a fixed, bounded structure. Use these headings in this order. Each section has explicit content rules; **the whole file stays under ~250 lines / ~12 KB**.
153	
154	```markdown
155	# Strategy
156	
157	## Goal
158	<two or three sentences naming the final theorem(s). NOT a paragraph of
159	motivation; just the destination. Cite by name, not by handwave.>
160	
161	## Phases & estimations
162	<one Markdown table, one row per remaining phase / route, rough order.
163	Columns: Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC (remaining · realized/it) | Key Mathlib needs | Risks.
164	The LOC cell carries TWO figures separated by `·`: the remaining-LOC
165	estimate (as before) AND the currently-realized velocity in that
166	direction — e.g. `≈250 · ~30/it`. Derive the velocity from the net
167	Lean LOC that ACTUALLY landed toward this phase over the last ~3 iters
168	(git diffstat / sorry-resolution deltas), not from hope. A phase
169	advancing little or nothing reads `≈250 · ~0/it` — that is a churning
170	signal, not a rounding artifact.
171	**Consistency check:** `remaining ÷ realized-per-it` should roughly
172	match the Iters-left cell. When it can't (e.g. 250 LOC remaining at
173	~30/it but Iters-left says 2), the estimate is fantasy — re-estimate
174	honestly (a mismatch this large is itself a >30% estimation change
175	that licenses editing the table).
176	Concise cells — one short line each. Drop rows for completed phases.
177	Aim for 4–10 rows.>
178	
179	## Routes
180	<only if the strategy admits multiple routes. One short subsection per
181	still-live route. Each: 3–6 lines naming the route, the pivot that
182	selected it, and the milestones marking its completion. NO Lean code,
183	NO blueprint excerpts. If single route, write "single route" here.>
184	
185	## Open strategic questions
186	<one-line bullets. Questions tracked but not yet decisions. Maximum ~8.
187	If you have more, you're using this as a scratchpad — move to iter sidecar.>
188	
189	## Mathlib gaps & new material
190	<one-line bullets, split into "Gaps to fill" (Mathlib pieces to build)
191	and "New project material" (defs/structures/lemmas introduced by the
192	project). Maximum ~12 total. Name the missing concept — NOT its
193	definition.>
194	```
195	
196	### Hard rules
197	
198	- **No Lean code, no blueprint excerpts, no proof sketches.** Those live in chapters.
199	- **No per-iter narrative.** No "this iter we tried X", no revision log. That history lives in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`.
200	- **No accumulation.** When a phase completes, delete its row. When a route is excised, remove its subsection. STRATEGY.md shrinks toward "complete"; it does NOT grow.
201	- **No long prose in table cells.** One short line per cell.
202	- **No "appendix" sections** (Historical decisions, Considered alternatives, Past iterations summary). Iter sidecars hold the alternatives that were rejected.
203	
204	### When to edit
205	
206	Edit STRATEGY.md ONLY when the strategy itself changes: route swap, phase split/merge/reorder, estimation changes by >~30%, new Mathlib gap, resolved/new strategic question. Otherwise leave it alone. A drifting velocity figure alone is NOT a reason to edit every iter — refresh it when you're already editing the table for one of the above, or when it exposes an Iters-left mismatch big enough to re-estimate.
207	
208	## Per-iteration sidecars
209	
210	The injected `## Per-iteration sidecars` block names where you write this iter's narrative (`iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`) and shows the last few iters' sidecars verbatim. Per-iter narrative goes there — not into STRATEGY.md, not into `task_pending.md`. `task_pending.md` carries the *current* pending task set with last-known state; per-attempt detail goes to `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md`.
211	
212	## Feasibility gate
213	
214	For difficult tasks: think harder. Align with `references/`. Use toy examples, analogies, alternative perspectives. Never delegate difficulty to "next iter" or "the prover".
215	
216	Question your previous work. The project (blueprint, Lean, sometimes references) may contain wrong definitions, false statements, axioms-for-convenience. If you identify a critical issue — new or long-present — address it. The catalog has subagents for restructuring; pick the appropriate one.
217	
218	For obstacles, decide whether Mathlib has the infrastructure or whether you need to fill a gap. Use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` for existence checks only — not proof exploration. For an external/alternative route, prefer the auto-injected subagent catalog (a literature/reference-fetching subagent will be listed there when enabled) or `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` directly. The `archon-informal-agent.py` tool can also generate a proof-style sketch when external LLM API credentials are configured in env (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`); verify availability with `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around it. The tool accepts `--provider auto` (default) to pick the best available key automatically. If filling a Mathlib gap is the only viable path, don't avoid it.
219	
220	## Stuck routes and deeper-think triggers
221	
222	Your catalog includes a [MANDATORY] convergence critic whose verdict is per active route. Build its directive from your own extracted signals (sorry counts per iter, helpers added per iter, prover statuses, recurring blocker phrases for the last K iters); read its descriptor for the directive format. Verdicts and the required response:
223	
224	- **CONVERGING / UNCLEAR** — proceed.
225	- **CHURNING** — STOP. Do not add more helpers. Execute the critic's named corrective this iter.
226	- **STUCK** — STOP. Route pivot is on the table; execute the corrective.
227	
228	If you believe the verdict is wrong, you may rebut it — but the rebuttal must be EXPLICIT in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`, citing the signals you disagree with and your alternative read. Silent overrides are forbidden. Silently assigning another helper round on a CHURNING route is the failure pattern the critic exists to prevent.
229	
230	Common correctives the critic names: expand the blueprint chapter, consult Mathlib idioms, refactor a load-bearing definition, pivot routes, or — for a strategy fork — decide it yourself and note the decision for the user to override. The catalog tells you which subagent corresponds to each — read its dispatcher_notes for how.
231	
232	**Make the call, then proceed — never defer the decision to the user.** When the critic (or your own analysis) surfaces a strategy fork, do NOT turn it into a blocking question. Choose the option best supported by the evidence and record it in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` under a `## Decision made` section: the option chosen, why, the LOC/risk trade-off you weighed, and the cheapest signal that would make you reverse it. Then dispatch provers on that option THIS iter. Review surfaces it in TO_USER.md as an FYI the user can override by adding a hint to `USER_HINTS.md`; if a contrary hint appears, the next plan agent revisits — otherwise the project simply keeps moving on your choice. What you must NEVER produce: a "no prover dispatch this iter — awaiting decision" round, an options menu with a "where to reply", or a "default to X if no reply" framing. The user is not on call; a question no one answers must not stall the loop.
233	
234	**Deeper-think trigger summary.** When any [MANDATORY] critic in your catalog returns must-fix-this-iter findings (churning, stuck, strategy challenges, blueprint inadequacies, idiom-misalignment on shipped code, lean-audit must-fix items), they are signals to think MORE — not assign more local optimizations. Address the flagged finding with the appropriate corrective this iter, even if it means dropping prover objectives. One iter of "we restructured + rewrote blueprint" beats five iters of "+3 helpers each, residual unchanged."
235	
236	## Subagent delegation
237	
238	Each subagent in your catalog is one tool. The catalog includes its description, write-domain hint, MANDATORY / read-only / can-spawn flags, and (under "Workflow guidance") its `dispatcher_notes` — *that's the canonical guidance for how to use that subagent*. Read the descriptor's full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` before composing the directive.
239	
240	### How to invoke
241	
242	Pick a kebab-case **slug** (each call within an iter must use a distinct slug — e.g. `split-wlocal`, `m1b-route`). Write the directive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md`, then run via the Bash tool (foreground, one call):
243	
244	```
245	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
246	  --name <subagent-name> \
247	  --slug <slug> \
248	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md \
249	  --write-domain '<glob>' \
250	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
251	```
252	
253	The wrapper prints a one-line status and exits 0 on success. `ARCHON_ITER_NUM` is set by the loop — no need to pass `--iter-num`.
254	
255	**Treat each dispatch as blocking.** Don't deliberately pass `run_in_background: true`. The wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it returns only once the child finishes and its report is written), but a dispatch is long-running, so the harness may auto-background it and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected. Either way, await the task / poll for the report at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` before you act on the result; never continue planning as if a still-running dispatch had already returned.
256	
257	**Directives must be fully self-contained.** Subagents do not read `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state; they read what you tell them to. Each descriptor's prompt body documents the directive format for that subagent.
258	
259	**Write-domain** globs constrain what the subagent (and any descendants it spawns) may modify. Common: `'Algebra/**'`, `'Algebra/WLocal.lean'`, `'task_results/**'` for read-only subagents. Children's declared domains must be a subset of yours.
260	
261	**Parallelism**: dispatch multiple subagents concurrently by issuing multiple Bash calls in one message. The dispatch semaphore caps total concurrent processes by `loop.max_parallel`.
262	
263	### After each subagent returns
264	
265	The subagent's report lands at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (or `task_results/<parent-slug>/<name>-<slug>.md` when nested). The loop auto-archives it to `logs/iter-NNN/` for the dashboard. You:
266	
267	1. **Read** the full report (the wrapper's stdout summary is compressed).
268	2. **Spot-check** load-bearing claims (the routine sorry-count / compile checks are already done by the loop).
269	3. **Update STRATEGY.md** if findings change the long-arc plan.
270	4. **Update PROGRESS.md** with whatever new objectives the report enables.
271	
272	### Canonical ordering
273	
274	Within a plan phase: read-only critics / precedent consults first, write-capable subagents next, verification / envelope subagents last. **Write prover objectives only after** the subagents have stabilized the definitional landscape.
275	
276	You may invoke a subagent multiple times per iter (distinct slugs each call) when justified.
277	
278	## Informal content for the prover
279	
280	The prover does much better with rich informal guidance. Before assigning a task, ensure the prover has access to the relevant informal proof.
281	
282	- **Short hints** (a few sentences): in `PROGRESS.md` under the objective.
283	- **Medium content** (a paragraph or two): delegate to the `lean-scaffolder` subagent to inject these into the corresponding `.lean` file as a `/- Blueprint note: ... -/` or `/- Planner strategy: ... -/` block above the target declaration. You MUST NOT edit `.lean` files yourself.
284	- **Long content** (full sketch, paper summary, multi-step construction): in the blueprint chapter `.tex`.
285	- **When a reference is vague**: actually consult the source before assigning the task. Options (pick whichever your environment provides):
286	  - The auto-injected subagent catalog — a literature/reference-fetching subagent surfaces there when enabled and downloads the original source files (PDF + TeX when available) into `references/`, each with a pointer `.md` index card.
287	  - `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` directly when you only need to confirm a paper exists or read a short passage.
288	  - `archon-informal-agent.py` to ask an external LLM for a proof-style sketch — **only when API credentials are configured** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`); use `--provider auto` and the script picks whichever key is available. The output is LLM-generated, NOT source-derived, so don't treat it as a literature cross-check.
289	  - Never send the prover in blind, and never synthesize a "literature cross-check" from your own context — see the anti-fabrication rule below.
290	
291	Always record in `PROGRESS.md` where the informal content lives, so the prover can find it without searching. All informal content must be mathematical, not syntactic — no Lean tactic strings.
292	
293	## Anti-fabrication rule (applies to all verification work)
294	
295	When a hint or strategy step asks for verification against an external source — literature cross-check, citation lookup, "consult the paper", "verify the construction matches Hartshorne III.6", a request to invoke a specific tool, etc. — and the named tool or path can't actually execute (missing API credentials, paywall, broken environment, source not found, tool reports `NOT_FOUND`), **you MUST NOT synthesize the verification output from your own context**. The planner's context is the same context that produced the claims being verified; a planner-written cross-check is circular by construction and worse than skipping the verification, because it disguises absence of verification as presence of it.
296	
297	The acceptable responses, in order of preference:
298	
299	1. **Substitute with an equivalent.** If the user named a specific tool (e.g. `archon-informal-agent.py`) but that tool can't run, look at the auto-injected subagent catalog above for a subagent that performs equivalent work (e.g. a literature/reference-fetching subagent for a literature request), or use `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` directly. Record the substitution in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` under a `## Tool substitutions` section so the user sees what you did and can correct the hint if the substitution is wrong.
300	
301	2. **Partial verification + honest scope.** If you can verify some claims but not others (some seeds resolve, some don't), surface that explicitly: which claims are verified, against which sources, and which remain unverified. The downstream blueprint-writer should cite only the verified ones; the unverified ones stay flagged.
302	
303	3. **Escalate to the user.** When neither substitution nor partial verification is possible, append a one-line bullet to `USER_HINTS.md` naming the specific failure (e.g. *"archon-informal-agent.py has no API credentials in env — please set `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` or another supported key, or rephrase the hint to allow a different tool"*), and proceed with the iter WITHOUT the verification, flagging in `PROGRESS.md` that this iter's strategic decisions affected by the missing verification are unverified.
304	
305	What you may NEVER do: write a file named `references/<topic>-crosscheck.md` (or similar) whose content is your own synthesis, dressed up to look like a verification report. If a future planner or prover treats that file as ground truth, they're acting on circular evidence the project has no way to detect or correct. This is the failure mode this rule exists to prevent — assume any "I'll just write it myself from what I remember" impulse is wrong, and use one of the three acceptable responses above instead.
306	
307	## Prover failure modes
308	
309	- **"Mathlib doesn't have it"** — the #1 failure. Do not pass it back with "try harder". Find an alternative route via the catalog (a literature/reference-fetching subagent when enabled), `WebSearch`/`WebFetch`, or `archon-informal-agent.py --provider auto` for a proof-style sketch when any API key is set (`env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"`). If the gap is in a definition, dispatch a write-capable structural subagent from your catalog. Update the chapter `.tex` with the re-routed proof before reassigning. For a **missing Mathlib lemma** (not a definition gap), see "Mathlib gradient strategy" below — build it project-side from available Mathlib, axiom-clean, rather than leaving a sorry gated on an upstream PR.
310	- **Wrong construction** — instruct revert (single file) or dispatch a structural subagent (cross-file). Update the chapter first.
311	- **Not using Web Search** — explicitly instruct: "use Web Search to find [arXiv ID], decompose into sub-lemmas, formalize step by step". Update the chapter with the retrieved sketch.
312	- **Early stop on a hard problem** — reject the report. Break into sub-goals in the chapter, assign L1, then L2 after L1 lands.
313	- **Tricks to bypass** (new axioms, ad-hoc weakenings) — reject. Document why this route was chosen and ensure it won't reproduce.
314	- **Repeated blockers** — same blocker over consecutive iters means rewrite the chapter or dispatch a structural subagent. Do NOT re-dispatch the same lane with cosmetic recipe variation.
315	
316	## "Owed iter-N+" rule
317	
318	**Do NOT write "owed iter-N+" in an objective when a recipe already exists** (in `analogies/`, the blueprint chapter, or a prior task result). That phrase signals the prover to stop after achieving the hard bar and not attempt the body. Writing it when a concrete proof sketch exists is artificial throttling — the exact thing the dispatch guidance says to avoid.
319	
320	Use "owed iter-N+" ONLY when the proof body has NO concrete route yet (the blueprint chapter is empty for that step, no analogies file exists, and the informal agent hasn't produced a usable sketch). In all other cases, write "attempt the body; recipe: <path/to/file> <section>" so the prover uses remaining budget on the body and leaves partial progress if stuck.
321	
322	Partial progress from a real attempt (a partial tactic block, a named sub-goal that compiles, a helper lemma that closes) is far more valuable to the next iter than a clean typed-sorry pin with no attempt. The prover stops when it's genuinely stuck, not when the hard-bar checkbox is ticked.
323	
324	## Mathlib gradient strategy
325	
326	When a sorry's body depends on a Mathlib lemma or definition that does not yet exist in Mathlib, the default response is to leave the body as a sorry and wait for a Mathlib upstream PR. This is slow and blocks all downstream work. Use the **Mathlib gradient** approach instead:
327	
328	1. **Identify the missing ingredient.** Name it precisely: "we need `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` for Dedekind extensions" or "we need `Finsupp.posPart` for ordered groups".
329	2. **Check if it's buildable from current Mathlib.** Use `archon-informal-agent.py` or `WebSearch` to find a proof using only today's Mathlib. Almost always possible for a single lemma.
330	3. **Assign the prover with `[prover-mode: mathlib-build]`** to formalize that single ingredient axiom-clean in the project file that needs it. One lemma per iter if needed. The mode's strict no-sorry invariant ensures the output is either clean code or a precise decomposition — no sorry pins. The goal: each step only uses Mathlib + things already axiom-clean in the project.
331	4. **Once the ingredient is axiom-clean, use `prove` mode to close the sorry** in the same or next iter.
332	
333	The invariant: every sorry body that is deferred must have EITHER (a) no known proof route (not yet in the literature, or requires genuinely novel mathematics) OR (b) a missing Mathlib ingredient that is itself the explicit next objective. A sorry gated on "waiting for Mathlib to add X" with no project-side build plan is a planning failure.
334	
335	This gradient approach converts the project from a chain of blocked sorries into a steady incremental flow where each iter adds axiom-clean content. It is especially important for algebraic geometry, where large swaths of Mathlib are absent — the project must build those swaths itself, one lemma at a time.
336	
337	## Verification
338	
339	The loop already runs deterministic checks each iter:
340	
341	- **Sorry count** — stamped into `meta.json` (before/mid/post prover). Do not re-count by hand.
342	- **Axiom check** — runs as part of the blueprint-doctor; new axioms surface in your injected findings block.
343	- **Blueprint consistency** — `sync_leanok` resolves `\lean{...}` against the project decls; the doctor catches broken `\ref` / `\uses`.
344	
345	What's left for you: spot-check inconsistent prover self-reports; act on every entry in the injected doctor findings (or document the deferral); reject any reported completion that left a real `sorry` or introduced a new axiom.
346	
347	## Decomposition strategy
348	
349	When a prover is stuck on a large theorem: read the chapter for sub-lemma structure (L1, L2, …); read related `references/` to align with the original proof; expand the chapter if too thin (dispatch a blueprint-writing or literature-fetching subagent from the catalog, or use `WebSearch`/`WebFetch` directly); assign one sub-lemma at a time; verify, then assign the next; record each sub-lemma's status in `PROGRESS.md`.
350	
351	## Soundness check before spending budget
352	
353	**Churning and unsoundness are different signals — the critic catches the first, only you catch the second.** A recurring blocker is not always a hard-but-true gap; sometimes the target `sorry` is a statement that is simply **false as written** (a missing hypothesis, a wrong quantifier, an unstated connectedness/finiteness assumption). Pouring prover budget into a false statement burns iterations forever — the prover correctly cannot close it, the progress-critic reports CHURNING, and everyone treats it as a Mathlib gap when the real fix is one word in the statement.
354	
355	So **before committing more than one iter of prover budget to a hard or recurring `sorry`** (a target the progress-critic flags as a repeated blocker, or any target you estimate at multiple iters / >~100 LOC), first spend a cheap pass trying to **DISPROVE** the statement:
356	
357	- Instantiate it on the smallest non-trivial models — finite, degenerate, or boundary cases (e.g. for an algebra claim: `B = k × k`, `B = ℚ(√2)`, the zero ring, a one-point space). Does any satisfy the hypotheses but violate the conclusion?
358	- If you can't see it yourself, dispatch the informal / mathlib-analogist subagent from your catalog with a directive that asks specifically for a **counterexample or a satisfiability sketch**, not a proof.
359	- Check whether the source the statement claims to follow actually states it with the same hypotheses (cite-and-read discipline applies — read the local source file).
360	
361	If a counterexample turns up, the statement (or a missing hypothesis) is the bug: fix the blueprint statement, mark the Lean declaration with a `% NOTE:` for review, and do NOT assign a prover to formalize the false version. If the disproof attempt fails, you've cheaply raised your confidence that the target is true — now spend the budget. Record the disproof attempt and its outcome in the iter sidecar so the next planner doesn't repeat it.
362	
363	## Prover modes
364	
365	The available prover modes and their selection criteria are injected above under **Available prover modes**. Each mode's `dispatcher_notes` tell you exactly when to use it.
366	
367	**Mode selection is a required step, not optional.** For every file you add to `## Current Objectives`:
368	
369	1. Read the `dispatcher_notes` for each available mode (injected above).
370	2. Pick the mode whose `dispatcher_notes` best match the file's situation.
371	3. If a non-default mode fits, tag the objective line: `[prover-mode: <name>]`.
372	4. If the default is correct, no tag is needed — but you must have consciously checked.
373	
374	## Multi-agent coordination
375	
376	Provers run in parallel — one per file. Number objectives clearly; each maps to exactly one `.lean` file. Reference the blueprint chapter alongside, and **list every ready sorry in that file that the prover should fill in this iter** — not just one:
377	
378	```markdown
379	## Current Objectives
380	
381	1. **`Core.lean`** — Fill sorries in `filter_convergence` (line 156), `filter_inv` (line 188), `filter_assoc` (line 211). Blueprint: `chapters/Core.tex` (`thm:filter_convergence`, `thm:filter_inv`, `thm:filter_assoc`).
382	2. **`Measure.lean`** — Fill sorry in `sigma_finite_restrict` (line 45). Blueprint: `chapters/Measure.tex`. [prover-mode: fine-grained]
383	3. **`ChartAlgebra.lean`** — Scaffold the file with declarations for `thm:chart_id`, `thm:chart_comp`, `thm:chart_inv` from the chapter; leave bodies as `sorry`. Blueprint: `chapters/ChartAlgebra.tex`. (File-skeleton dispatch — see below.)
384	```
385	
386	**Agent count = file count.** The dispatcher fans out one prover per file. When a file has multiple ready sorries, list ALL of them under that file's objective — the prover handles them sequentially within one lane. Splitting a multi-sorry file across iters is artificial throttling; one lane working sequentially on three sorries finishes faster than waiting two iters for three single-sorry lanes (the prover keeps its file context warm across sorries).
387	
388	**File-skeleton dispatches.** When a blueprint chapter is complete but the corresponding `.lean` file does not yet exist (or exists but is missing declarations the chapter introduced), it is a legitimate iter objective to dispatch a prover with directive *"scaffold `Foo.lean` with declarations for `thm:a`, `thm:b`, `thm:c` from the chapter; leave bodies as `sorry`; add the import + namespace boilerplate; do not attempt to prove anything yet"*. The next iter then fills the sorries. This is materially faster than one-iter-per-declaration scaffolding.
389	
390	**Mechanical-vs-deep partition.** Sorries split into two regimes:
391	- *Mechanical* — typeclass wiring, instance synthesis, ring-level algebra, simp/ring-tactic territory, definitional unfolding glue. A prover lane can comfortably close 3–6 of these in one iter (the attempt-cap permits each one a fresh budget). When the upcoming work is mechanical, load lanes aggressively.
392	- *Deep* — the load-bearing categorical / geometric / analytic argument. One per lane, often less. The prover may also legitimately spend an iter exploring without closing anything.
393	
394	Use this partition to decide how thickly to load each lane. Don't load a deep lane with three deep sorries; that just thrashes the prover's attempt budget across all three. Don't restrict a mechanical lane to one sorry "to keep it simple"; the prover wants the batched objectives.
395	
396	Balance difficulty so all provers finish around the same time. Avoid shallow / trivial objectives unless they unblock something downstream this iter. Don't artificially throttle — the prover prompt says "push as far as possible"; your objective list must give it room to.
397	
398	If a previous experiment is being restarted, check compilation status of every target `.lean` first. Prioritize files with sorries or compile errors; don't redo completed work.
399	
400	**Dispatch cap.** The runner refuses to fan out more than ~10 provers in a single iter (configurable via `--max-objectives`, default 10). Writing 15+ files into `## Current Objectives` is a planning failure, not a tooling limitation — even when the project has many open files, the right iter-level move is to pick the most urgent ≤10 (mechanical lanes counted) and defer the rest to the next iter. If the deterministic plan-validate guard truncates your list, the surplus is added to `USER_HINTS.md` so the next planner sees what got deferred. Don't rely on the safety net — pick the right ~10 the first time.
401	
402	**Blocked-deps filter.** Before dispatch, plan-validate also drops any objective whose transitive local imports failed the *previous* `lake build`. Reason: a prover assigned to `Downstream.lean` that imports `Upstream.lean`-which-doesn't-compile would fail to even load the file, burning API time for nothing. The blocked set is parsed from `.archon/last_lake_build.log`. There is one important exception: a blocked file that's *itself* an objective this iter is presumed-being-fixed — the planner is allowed to assign `Upstream.lean` and `Downstream.lean` together (the prover phase handles them in import order). When the filter drops files, they're listed in `USER_HINTS.md` with their specific blocking deps, so you can prioritize fixing the upstream files next iter. Best practice: when you see `## Build state` flagging compile errors, put those files at the top of `## Current Objectives` so the filter exempts the downstream lanes that depend on them.
403	
404	## Dependency graph
405	
406	Optional but cheap. Before scoping objectives, you may run:
407	
408	```
409	${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" . --format=summary
410	```
411	
412	It parses imports + chapter `\lean{...}` / `\uses{...}` / `\proves{...}` / markers and emits a project-wide view in under a second. Use it to order objectives — upstream files first, downstream files later.
413	
414	## Stage transitions
415	
416	Advance `PROGRESS.md` when all current-stage objectives are met:
417	
418	- `autoformalize` → `prover` (all statements formalized)
419	- `prover` → `polish` (all sorries filled and verified)
420	- `polish` → `COMPLETE` (proofs clean, compile)

---STRATEGY---
# Strategy

## Goal

Formalize Christian Merten's Jacobian challenge (`references/challenge.lean.ref`): the nine
protected declarations headed by `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian` and
`Jacobian.nonempty_jacobianWitness` — an Albanese/Jacobian object uniform over the `k`-rational
pointing of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C/k` (`[Field k]` only; no `C(k)≠∅`,
no `CharZero`). `J := Pic⁰_{C/k}` is built unconditionally; only `isAlbaneseFor` is quantified over
the pointing. End-state: zero inline `sorry` in the dependency cone of each protected decl, 0
project axioms, kernel-only axioms. Spine: pointed vs. unpointed.

Posture is **option (c)**: forward the Route-A Picard substrate while Riemann–Roch stays frozen by
the USER ROUTE C PAUSE. The nine decls typecheck modulo named sorry-axioms (0 project axioms; none
yet kernel-clean).

## Phases & estimations

| Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC (rem · /it) | Key Mathlib needs | Risks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A.1.c.SubT — ⊗-group law (carrier `IsInvertible`; inverse free) | sole remaining bottleneck = d.2 stalk-tensor for the UNCONDITIONAL associator | ~4–7 | ~250–450 · ~0/it | d.2 varying-ring stalk-tensor `(M⊗ᵖN)_x≅M_x⊗_{R_x}N_x`; by-hand `CommGroup`; re-base `OnProduct`/RPF onto `IsInvertible` | d.2 is a ~200–400 LOC Mathlib-gap stalk port (filtered-colimit-of-tensors); flat-restriction route is FALSE (no global sectionwise flatness), Mathlib-monoidal route absent |
| A.1.c — RelPic functor | held (placeholder bodies) | ~3–5 | ~50–150 · 0/it | iso-class group; ét-sheafify on `Over S` | RE-ENGAGE: replace dishonest `PicSharp := const PUnit` + `functorial := 0` |
| A.2.c — representability (scaffolding) | priority-2 | ~12–16 | ~600–800 · 0/it | A.1.c | `⟨sorry⟩` constructors discharged only by the engine below |
| A.2.c-engine — Quot/Cartier (RR-free) | HOLD; first ungated sub-lane being seeded for parallelism: `Cohomology_FlatBaseChange` (i=0 flat base change, Stacks 02KH) | ~30–60 | ~3400–5500 · 0/it | R^i f_* (i≥1), Relative Proj, CM-regularity, flattening | largest build; 2–4× cheaper RR route behind the pause; engine-coverage seeding is the USER parallelism work |
| A.3 — tangent + Pic⁰ AV-structure | gated A.2.c | ~26–45 | ~1100–2100 · 0/it | scheme tangent space; Hilbert poly | absent in Mathlib; likely under-counted |
| A.4 — Albanese UP (Route 2) | gated A.2.c; autoduality RR-freeness UNVERIFIED | ~12–20 | ~600–1000 · 0/it | `rmk:Alb` + autoduality `J^∨≅J` + Galois descent | if autoduality needs RR, collides with Route C |
| genusZero + witness body | gated A.3 | ~5–7 | ~250–450 · 0/it | tangent-iso + connectedness | hidden A.2.c transit |

**Total Route A**: ~95–180 iters / ~4300–7500 LOC (RR-free engine path). The substrate's sole ungated
bottleneck is now d.2 (the varying-ring stalk-tensor, ~200–400 LOC); the engine foundations run in
parallel. Escalation-to-user is DISABLED (USER autonomous-operation directive): the loop decides the
route autonomously and may refactor a dead-end. The RR-fork remains an FYI only.

## Routes

`J := Pic⁰_{C/k}` (Kleiman §4–5, Nitsure §5, Milne III §6). Bottom-up (USER): ungated roots first,
no gated target before its roots, no A.3+ before A.2.c. Every directive cites
Kleiman/Nitsure/Milne/Mumford/Hartshorne/Stacks. **Critical path (RR-free):** A.1.c.SubT →
A.1.c → A.2.c.

**A.1.c.SubT — group law via the tensor-invertibility carrier.** `Pic X` = the commutative group of
iso-classes of **invertible** `𝒪_X`-modules under ⊗, where invertible means tensor-invertible:
`IsInvertible M := ∃ N, Nonempty (M ⊗ N ≅ 𝒪_X)` (Stacks 0B8M/01CX — the definition of an invertible
module; equivalent to locally-free-rank-1 on any ringed space). Carrying the group on THIS predicate
makes the group axioms near-trivial: identity `[𝒪_X]`; closure from associator+braiding; **inverse
`[M]⁻¹ := [N]` is the membership witness — free, no inverse object to construct**; inverse
well-defined since `N` is determined up to iso (`N ≅ N⊗𝒪 ≅ N⊗(M⊗N') ≅ 𝒪⊗N' ≅ N'`). `CommRing.Pic`
(= `Skeleton (Module.Invertible R)`) is NOT reusable (it reads its group off a coherent
`MonoidalCategory` over a FIXED ring, unavailable for the varying `𝒪_X`), so the `CommGroup` is built
by hand from existence-of-iso lemmas; pentagon/coherence is never invoked. The restriction linchpin
`tensorObj_restrict_iso`, unitors, and braiding are axiom-clean.
Carrying the group on invertibility deletes the inverse-object manufacture (the internal-hom dual
`Scheme.Modules.dual` / cocycle gluing): the inverse is the witness, so the entire dual / internal-hom
apparatus drops off the group's critical path. This is a strict reduction of remaining work — the dual is
deleted, not renamed.
**Sole remaining bottleneck = d.2.** `mul_assoc` needs `tensorObj_assoc_iso`. The presheaf-level
associator (`PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator`) exists for ALL modules; the only gap
is the sheafification transport, whose single open obligation is `isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W`
(`F ◁ toSheafify ∈ J.W`). This closes UNCONDITIONALLY via **d.2**: the varying-ring stalk-tensor
commutation `(M ⊗ᵖ N)_x ≅ M_x ⊗_{R_x} N_x` (a `J.W`-morphism is a stalkwise iso, and `(F◁g)_x = id⊗g_x`
is then an iso). d.2 is a bounded ~200–400 LOC Mathlib-gap stalk port (filtered colimit of tensors over
the colimit ring; d.1 `stalkLinearMap` already built). Once d.2 lands, the associator is unconditional,
the whiskering apparatus retires, and `thm:pic_commgroup` closes (inverse free; unitors/braiding clean).
**Rejected associator routes:** (a) flat-restriction (invertible⟹flat) is FALSE — local-freeness does
NOT give global sectionwise flatness on a non-affine open (iter-212 finding); (b) reading the associator
off Mathlib's monoidal-sheafification is unavailable — `Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW` is not in Mathlib,
`Sheaf.monoidalCategory` is for a fixed value category not `SheafOfModules` over a varying ring, and the
only `J.W.IsMonoidal` instance needs `MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules)`, strictly more work
(`analogies/monoidal-transport.md`).
**Other remaining work.** Re-base the relative-Picard consumer (`LineBundle.OnProduct`, `RelPicFunctor`,
`addCommGroup_via_tensorObj`) from `IsLocallyTrivial` onto `IsInvertible` (cheap — unbuilt stubs).
`IsInvertible ⟺ IsLocallyTrivial` (Stacks 0B8M) stays a deferred bridge (the group law no longer needs
it, since the associator is unconditional).
**Deferred (off critical path):** dual object + C-bridge `dual_restrict_iso` + `exists_tensorObj_inverse`.
**Dead ends:** flat-restriction associator; routing the dual base-change through `overSliceSheafEquiv`;
the sheafify-the-presheaf associator/eval shortcut; the Mathlib monoidal-sheafification shortcut.

**A.2.c — representability + Quot fork (held).** Six Prop-valued typeclasses with `⟨sorry⟩`
constructors scaffold representability (~600–800 LOC); Route A proceeds under them. Discharge fork:
RR-free general Quot/Hilbert engine (Nitsure §5 + Kleiman §4, ~3400–5500 LOC, all Mathlib-absent;
deepest root `R^i f_*`, i≥1) vs cheap curve route (Kleiman §5, `Sym^n`/Abel–Jacobi, ~600–1000 LOC,
needs paused RR). Engine HELD behind A.1.c.SubT→A.1.c.

**Albanese UP — Route 2.** UP from `Pic` representability via Kleiman `rmk:Alb` (RR-free) on the
dual `J^∨`, landed on `J` by autoduality `J^∨≅J` (Milne 6.6 / EGK 2.1) + Galois descent `k̄→k`

three Goal nodes; would unlock the cheap curve route. Pause cost: the ~3400+ LOC engine and the
autoduality risk exist solely to avoid RR.

rigidity — substrate partial, PAUSED (USER).

100	### Proof-sketch depth
101	**Under-specified** for `lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso`.
102	
103	The proof sketch has four movements (sections agree on top; comparison ring map is φ; assemble via tilde full-faithfulness; scalar compatibility). The sketch reads as if it closes the proof, but there is a **circular dependency** in movement 3:
104	
105	> The chapter's remark says: "QC of the pushforward. The tilde of any module is quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of Spec(R)-modules is closed under isomorphism; hence (Spec φ)_*(M~) is quasi-coherent. In particular no separate 'pushforward preserves quasi-coherent' theorem is needed."
106	
107	This argument uses the object isomorphism `(Spec φ)_*(M~) ≅ (restr_φ M)~` (already established in movement 3) to conclude QC of the pushforward by closure under iso. But the Lean formalization reveals the actual dependency order is **inverted**: the object iso is built via `fromTildeΓ (pushforward (M~)) : tilde(Γ(pushforward(M~))) ⟶ pushforward(M~)`, and this map is an iso **if and only if** `pushforward(M~)` is already quasi-coherent. QC of the pushforward is therefore a **prerequisite** for movement 3, not a corollary of it.
108	
109	The Lean inline comment (lines 236–243) explicitly identifies quasi-coherence of the pushforward as the "sole remaining obligation" and lists three non-trivial alternative routes (SheafOfModules.Presentation; `IsQuasicoherent.of_coversTop` on basic opens; direct `isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis` via `IsLocalizedModule`). The blueprint's remark contains a circular argument that would confuse a future prover trying to implement movement 3.
110	
111	**Recommended fix**: The remark should be rewritten to flag QC of the pushforward as a prerequisite (the hard part), and the proof sketch should describe one of the non-circular routes (e.g., the `IsLocalizedModule` on basic opens strategy).
112	
113	### Hint precision
114	**Precise** for all pinned declarations. The `\lean{...}` hints name the exact Lean declaration names; all signatures match what the prose says.
115	
116	### Generality
117	**Matches need** — `gammaPushforwardIso` is stated for general `N : (Spec R').Modules` (not just tilde-modules), which is the right level; `gammaPushforwardTildeIso` then specialises. If/when these get blueprint blocks, the general form should be the primary one.
118	
119	### Recommended chapter-side actions (for blueprint-writing subagent)
120	1. **Add a `\begin{lemma} ... \end{lemma}` block for `globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop`** between the "locality of isomorphisms" subsection and the "affine computation" subsection. Lean hint: `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}`. Informal statement: the ring square `gsR.hom ≫ (Spec φ).appTop = φ ≫ gsR'.hom` commutes, where `gsR = StructureSheaf.globalSectionsIso R`.
121	2. **Add a `\begin{definition} ... \end{definition}` (or lemma) block for `gammaPushforwardIso`** immediately before `lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso`. Lean hint: `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}`. Informal statement: for any `N : (Spec R').Modules`, the `R`-module `Γ((Spec φ)_* N)` is canonically isomorphic to `restrictScalars φ (Γ N)` as `R`-modules. Proof sketch: both sides peel by `rfl` to nested `restrictScalars` towers; reconcile via `restrictScalarsComp'App` × 2 and the ring equation from the above lemma.
122	3. **Add a `\begin{definition} ... \end{definition}` (or lemma) block for `gammaPushforwardTildeIso`** as a corollary of `gammaPushforwardIso`. Lean hint: `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardTildeIso}`. Informal statement: for the tilde specialisation `N = M~`, compose `gammaPushforwardIso` with the unit iso `Γ(M~) ≅ M` to get `Γ((Spec φ)_*(M~)) ≅ restrictScalars φ M`.
123	4. **Fix the circular dependency in the remark after `lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso`**: replace "QC is closed under iso; hence the pushforward is QC" with a statement that QC of the pushforward must be established FIRST by an independent route (Presentation, IsLocalizedModule, or basic-open locality), and that closure-under-iso only applies thereafter.

240	open MonoidalCategory CategoryTheory.Presheaf
241	
242	variable {J : CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology C}
243	
244	/-- **Whiskering a locally bijective morphism preserves local injectivity
245	(flatness-free).** For an *arbitrary* presheaf of modules `F` and a morphism `g`
246	whose underlying additive-presheaf map is locally bijective (`∈ J.W`), the
247	left-whiskered morphism `F ◁ g` is locally injective.
248	
249	This is the single residual ingredient of the associator
250	`tensorObj_assoc_iso` under ROUTE (d). The mathematics is the stalkwise computation
251	`(F ◁ g)_x = id_{F_x} ⊗_{R_x} g_x`: a `J.W`-morphism on the topological site of `X`
252	is a *stalkwise isomorphism* (`TopCat.hasEnoughPoints` + the conservative-family
253	characterisation `hP.W_iff`, `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.*`, 2026), and
254	tensoring an isomorphism `g_x` by `id_{F_x}` yields an isomorphism, so `F ◁ g` is a
255	stalkwise iso, hence locally bijective, hence locally injective — for *any* `F`,
256	needing NEITHER sectionwise flatness NOR local triviality of `F`.
257	
258	The two Mathlib-side ingredients this stalkwise argument needs are
259	(d.1) the stalkwise characterisation of the module-level `J.W` on `Opens X` and
260	(d.2) the commutation `(A ⊗ᵖ B)_x ≅ A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x` of the stalk with the
261	presheaf-of-modules tensor (stalk = filtered colimit, and `tensorLeft`/`tensorRight`
262	preserve filtered colimits over a module category). Neither ships at the
263	`PresheafOfModules` level at the pinned commit; porting them is the genuine residual
264	(analogist `ts-monoidal213.md`, Decision 3, porting ingredients d.1/d.2). Stated
265	here with its substantive intended type so the rest of the associator closes against
266	it; see the task result for the precise missing Mathlib statements. -/
267	lemma isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W [J.WEqualsLocallyBijective Ab.{u}]
268	    (F : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _))
269	    {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)} (g : M ⟶ N)
270	    (hg : J.W ((toPresheaf _).map g)) :
271	    IsLocallyInjective J (F ◁ g) := by
272	  -- ROUTE (e) residual: `(F ◁ g)_x = id_{F_x} ⊗ g_x` is an iso since `g_x` is
273	  -- (a `J.W`-map is a stalkwise iso); tensoring by `id` of an iso is flatness-free.
274	  --
275	  -- iter-214 CORRECTION (Step 0 + d.1 partial): the module-level stalk is NOT
276	  -- Mathlib-absent — `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk` supplies, for
277	  -- `X : TopCat`, `R : X.Presheaf CommRingCat`, the stalk module
278	  -- `Module (R.stalk x) ↑(TopCat.Presheaf.stalk M.presheaf x)` and `germ_smul`. The
279	  -- linear-stalk-map packaging (ingredient d.1) is now built project-side:
280	  -- `PresheafOfModules.stalkLinearMap` (+ `stalkLinearMap_germ`,
281	  -- `stalkLinearMap_bijective_of_isIso`), all axiom-clean.
282	  --
283	  -- The TWO residual gaps to close this sorry (both require SPECIALISING this lemma
284	  -- to the topological site `C = Opens X` — the general-site statement here has no
285	  -- stalks; decl is UNPROTECTED so the specialisation is free, and the only consumer
286	  -- `tensorObj_assoc_iso` already works over `Opens.grothendieckTopology X`):
287	  --   (d.1-bridge) `(Opens.grothendieckTopology X).W (toPresheaf-image) ↔ ∀ x,
288	  --     IsIso (stalkFunctor Ab x map)` — assemble from `HasEnoughPoints
289	  --     (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)` (`Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/Points.lean`) +
290	  --     `W_iff`, OR from `WEqualsLocallyBijective` + the TopCat criteria
291	  --     `locally_surjective_iff_surjective_on_stalks` /
292	  --     `app_injective_iff_stalkFunctor_map_injective`
293	  --     (needs `presheafFiber ≅ TopCat.Presheaf.stalk`, the Mathlib TODO bridge).
294	  --   (d.2) stalk ⊗ commutation `(F ⊗ᵖ M)_x ≅ F_x ⊗_{R_x} M_x` naturally identifying
295	  --     `(F ◁ g)_x` with `LinearMap.lTensor F_x (stalkLinearMap g x)` — "tensor
296	  --     commutes with the filtered colimit defining the stalk" over the varying ring;
297	  --     genuinely Mathlib-absent (largest piece). Then `stalkLinearMap_bijective_of_isIso`
298	  --     + `LinearEquiv.lTensor` finish (flatness-free). See task result for full decomp.
299	  sorry
300	
301	/-- **Flatness-free left-whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer.** The
302	ROUTE (d) replacement for `W_whiskerLeft_of_flat`: for an *arbitrary* `F` and a
303	locally bijective `g` (`∈ J.W`), the left-whiskered `F ◁ g` again lies in `J.W`.
304	Local surjectivity is free (`isLocallySurjective_whiskerLeft`, right-exactness);
305	local injectivity is the flatness-free stalkwise residual
306	`isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W`. No flatness, no local triviality. -/
307	lemma W_whiskerLeft_of_W [J.WEqualsLocallyBijective Ab.{u}]
308	    (F : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _))
309	    {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)} (g : M ⟶ N)
310	    (hg : J.W ((toPresheaf _).map g)) :
311	    J.W ((toPresheaf _).map (F ◁ g)) := by
312	  have hbij := hg
313	  rw [GrothendieckTopology.W_iff_isLocallyBijective] at hbij
314	  rw [GrothendieckTopology.W_iff_isLocallyBijective]
315	  exact ⟨isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W F g hg,
316	    isLocallySurjective_whiskerLeft F g hbij.2⟩
317	
318	/-- **Flatness-free right-whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer.** The
319	braiding-conjugate of `W_whiskerLeft_of_W`, mirroring `W_whiskerRight_of_flat`. -/
320	lemma W_whiskerRight_of_W [J.WEqualsLocallyBijective Ab.{u}]
321	    (F : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _))
322	    {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)} (g : M ⟶ N)
323	    (hg : J.W ((toPresheaf _).map g)) :
324	    J.W ((toPresheaf _).map (g ▷ F)) := by
325	  have hwl := W_whiskerLeft_of_W F g hg
326	  have hconj : g ▷ F
327	      = (BraidedCategory.braiding M F).hom ≫ (F ◁ g) ≫ (BraidedCategory.braiding N F).inv := by
328	    rw [← Category.assoc, ← BraidedCategory.braiding_naturality_left g F, Category.assoc,
329	      Iso.hom_inv_id, Category.comp_id]
330	  rw [hconj, Functor.map_comp, Functor.map_comp]
331	  rw [(J.W).cancel_left_of_respectsIso, (J.W).cancel_right_of_respectsIso]
332	  exact hwl
333	
334	end WhiskerOfW
335	
336	/-- **The sheafification-localization bridge.** A morphism `f` of presheaves of
337	modules whose underlying additive-presheaf map lies in the sheafification localizer
338	`J.W` is sent by the associated-sheaf-of-modules functor to an isomorphism. This is
339	the single residual of the `⊗`-invertibility associator
340	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso`. It is the morphism-property
341	identity `PresheafOfModules.inverseImage_W_toPresheaf_eq_inverseImage_isomorphisms`
342	(the sheafification functor *is* the localization at `J.W.inverseImage (toPresheaf _)`)
343	read at a single morphism. -/
344	lemma isIso_sheafification_map_of_W
345	    {R₀ : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat} {Rsh : CategoryTheory.Sheaf J RingCat} (α : R₀ ⟶ Rsh.obj)
346	    [Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective J α] [Presheaf.IsLocallySurjective J α]
347	    [J.WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat] [CategoryTheory.HasWeakSheafify J AddCommGrpCat]
348	    {A B : PresheafOfModules.{u} R₀} (f : A ⟶ B)
349	    (hf : J.W ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf R₀).map f)) :
350	    IsIso ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification α).map f) := by
351	  have h := PresheafOfModules.inverseImage_W_toPresheaf_eq_inverseImage_isomorphisms (J := J) α
352	  have h2 : (CategoryTheory.MorphismProperty.isomorphisms (SheafOfModules Rsh)).inverseImage
353	      (PresheafOfModules.sheafification α) f := by rw [← h]; exact hf
354	  exact h2
355	
356	end FlatWhisker
357	
358	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — the `R.stalk x`-linear stalk map
359	(ROUTE (e), ingredient d.1)
360	
361	The route-(e) residual `isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W` is a stalkwise
362	argument: a `J.W`-morphism `g` is a *stalkwise isomorphism*, so `(F ◁ g)_x =
363	id_{F_x} ⊗_{R_x} g_x` is again an isomorphism for arbitrary `F`. The stalkwise
364	characterisation it ultimately rests on (ingredient d.1) requires the induced
365	Ab-stalk map of a morphism `g : M ⟶ N` of presheaves of `R`-modules to be packaged
366	as an **`R.stalk x`-linear map** between the stalk modules.
367	
368	Mathlib (`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk`) already supplies, for `X : TopCat`
369	and `R : X.Presheaf CommRingCat`, the stalk module structure
370	`Module (R.stalk x) ↑(TopCat.Presheaf.stalk M.presheaf x)` together with the germ /
371	scalar compatibility `PresheafOfModules.germ_smul`; what it does **not** supply is the
372	linearity of the induced stalk map `(stalkFunctor Ab x).map ((toPresheaf _).map g)`.
373	This section provides that packaging (the first concrete ingredient of d.1 toward
374	`isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W`). The base ring presheaf is necessarily
375	`CommRingCat`-valued, matching the project's `X.presheaf` carrier. -/
376	
377	section StalkLinearMap
378	
379	open TopologicalSpace TopCat.Presheaf Opposite
380	
381	variable {X : TopCat.{u}} {R : X.Presheaf CommRingCat.{u}}
382	
383	/-- **The `R.stalk x`-linear stalk map of a morphism of presheaves of modules.**
384	For `g : M ⟶ N` in `PresheafOfModules (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)` over a topological space
385	`X` and a point `x`, the induced Ab-stalk map `(stalkFunctor Ab x).map
386	((toPresheaf _).map g) : M.presheaf.stalk x ⟶ N.presheaf.stalk x` is `R.stalk x`-linear
387	for the stalk module structures of `Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk`.
388	Project-local: Mathlib packages the stalk module structure (`germ_smul`) but not the
389	linearity of the induced stalk map. This is ingredient (d.1) of the route-(e)
390	stalkwise argument for `isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W`. -/
391	noncomputable def stalkLinearMap
392	    {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)} (g : M ⟶ N) (x : X) :
393	    (↑(TopCat.Presheaf.stalk M.presheaf x) : Type u) →ₗ[↑(R.stalk x)]
394	      (↑(TopCat.Presheaf.stalk N.presheaf x) : Type u) where
395	  toFun := (ConcreteCategory.hom
396	    ((TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor AddCommGrpCat.{u} x).map ((toPresheaf _).map g)))
397	  map_add' a b := map_add _ a b
398	  map_smul' r s := by
399	    dsimp only [RingHom.id_apply]

The critical path is now clear: `stalkTensorIso` is built but not yet wired into `isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W` (Vestigial.lean:299 `sorry`), which gates `tensorObj_assoc_iso` → the by-hand `CommGroup` → `thm:pic_commgroup`. Vestigial.lean is **not** in the `covers` line (only TensorObjSubstrate.lean + StalkTensor.lean). The whiskering lemma it contains is documented in the consolidated chapter but the file is uncovered. Let me read the whiskering/d.1-bridge blueprint section and the live lemma block.

1095	Hence the stalk ingredients (d.1)/(d.2) are \emph{on} the critical path of the group
1096	law --- through the associator's single whisker obligation --- even though the full
1097	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category on all of
1098	\(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) (the subject of the present section) is \emph{not} built:
1099	that full monoidal generality, with its \emph{pair} of whiskering-stability fields
1100	(\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}) and all derived coherence, is what the group law does not
1101	consume. The single open obligation of the present section's full-monoidal target is
1102	thus the local-injectivity half packaged for an arbitrary whisker object
1103	(\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}), itself a consequence of the same d.2
1104	commutation.
1105	
1106	The following lemma is the \(R_x\)-linear stalk-map packaging (the ``d.1''
1107	ingredient). It is consumed by the stalk--tensor commutation of
1108	\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor} (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation,
1109	lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}) and is therefore a live ingredient
1110	of the unconditional associator route.
1111	
1112	\begin{lemma}
1113	% Live ingredient: consumed by lem:stalk_tensor_commutation and
1114	% lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk (sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor).
1115	
1116	[The $R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x$-linear stalk map of a morphism of presheaves of modules]
1117	  \label{lem:stalk_linear_map}
1118	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1119	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(R\) a presheaf of commutative rings on
1120	  \(X\), and \(x \in X\) a point. For a morphism \(g : M \to N\) of presheaves of
1121	  \(R\)-modules, the induced map on stalks
1122	  \[
1123	    g_x \;\colon\; M_x \;\longrightarrow\; N_x
1124	  \]
1125	  is \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\)), and is
1126	  characterised on germs by
1127	  \(g_x(\mathrm{germ}_x\,s) = \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,s)\)
1128	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_germ}\)). When the underlying map of
1129	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued stalks is an isomorphism, \(g_x\) is bijective
1130	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\)); in that case it bundles to
1131	  an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear equivalence
1132	  \(M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\) (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso}\)).
1133	
1134	  These four declarations are the \emph{(d.1)-partial implementation} --- the
1135	  \(R_x\)-linearity packaging of stalk maps --- consumed by the
1136	  \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step in the proofs of
1137	  \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation} and
1138	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}. They build on the
1139	  stalk module structure of \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk};
1140	  the linearity packaging itself is project-original.
1141	\end{lemma}
1142	
1143	\begin{proof}
1144	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1145	  The stalk \(M_x = \mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) carries its
1146	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure from
1147	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk}. The underlying additive map
1148	  \(g_x\) on stalks is the filtered colimit of the section maps \(g_U\); since
1149	  each section map is \(R(U)\)-linear and the scalar action on the stalk is the
1150	  colimit of the section actions, \(g_x\) respects the
1151	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-action, which gives the linear map together with its
1152	  germ characterisation. Bijectivity is read off the underlying
1153	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk isomorphism (a bijective linear map is a linear
1154	  equivalence), and bundling the inverse yields the linear equivalence.
1155	\end{proof}
1156	
1157	\emph{Off-path duplicate --- the full-generality packaging, not to be formalized.}
1158	The following lemma is the load-bearing whiskering-stability field of the \emph{full}
1159	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal-category route
1160	to the associator. That \emph{full monoidal category} is not built: the group law on
1161	iso-classes consumes only the \emph{existence} of the associator
1162	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The associator instead arises as the
1163	sheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the
1164	live lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}. The stalk ingredients
1165	(d.1)/(d.2) themselves --- the \(R_x\)-linear stalk map \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}
1166	and the stalk--tensor commutation \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation} --- are
1167	\emph{on} the critical path through that live lemma; what is off path is only the
1168	\emph{full-generality} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) packaging of the following
1169	duplicate, retained for the historical record.
1170	
1171	\begin{lemma}
1172	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1173	
1174	\leanok
1175	[Local injectivity of a whiskered localizer morphism (stalkwise, flatness-free)]
1176	  \label{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1177	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1178	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
1179	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the sheafification localizer (the locally
1180	  bijective morphisms). Concretely the site carries the
1181	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) property for \(\mathtt{Ab}\), so \(J.W\) is
1182	  exactly the class of \(J\)-locally bijective morphisms; this is recorded as a
1183	  typeclass hypothesis \([J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}]\) on
1184	  the statement. Let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of
1185	  \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of
1186	  \(R\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
1187	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R).\mathrm{map}\,g\) lies in \(J.W\). Then the
1188	  left-whiskered morphism
1189	  \[
1190	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
1191	  \]
1192	  is \emph{\(J\)-locally injective}. No flatness and no local-triviality
1193	  hypothesis on \(F\) is required.
1194	
1195	  This is the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\)-field content (the injectivity half) of the
1196	  \emph{full} instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), the
1197	  load-bearing field of the off-path \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal-category
1198	  path (arbitrary \(F\)). Its content for an arbitrary \(F\) is exactly the live lemma
1199	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, which the associator's
1200	  sheafification transport consumes and which is closed \emph{unconditionally} by the
1201	  d.2 stalk--tensor commutation (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}); the d.1/d.2
1202	  ingredients are therefore on the critical path. The two proofs recorded below --- a
1203	  sheaf-level argument specialised to locally trivial \(F\), and the general stalkwise
1204	  argument via (d.1)/(d.2) --- are retained for the historical record; the live group
1205	  law closes this obligation in its general (d.2) form through
1206	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}.
1207	\end{lemma}
1208	
1209	\begin{proof}
1210	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map,
1211	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1212	
1213	  \emph{PRIMARY route (\(F\) locally trivial --- the consumer's case; sheaf-level,
1214	  no stalks, no (d.2)).} Suppose \(F\) is locally trivial
1215	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
1216	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
1217	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
1218	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
1219	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
1220	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).
1221	  On such a \(V\):
1222	  \begin{itemize}
1223	    \item the substrate--restriction compatibility \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1224	      gives a canonical isomorphism
1225	      \[
1226	        (F \triangleleft g)\big|_V \;\cong\; (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)
1227	      \]
1228	      --- restriction along the open immersion \(V \hookrightarrow X\) commutes with
1229	      \(\otimes\), and the whiskering \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\)
1230	      is \(\otimes\) with an identity, so it restricts to \(\mathrm{id}_{F|_V}
1231	      \otimes (g|_V) = (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)\);
1232	    \item the trivialisation \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) together with the left
1233	      unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_V \otimes_V (-) \cong
1234	      (-)\)) identifies \((F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V) \cong g|_V\).
1235	  \end{itemize}
1236	  Composing, \((F \triangleleft g)|_V\) is isomorphic to \(g|_V\). Now \(g \in J.W\),
1237	  and membership in \(J.W\) (local bijectivity) is stable under restriction along an
1238	  open immersion, so \(g|_V \in J.W\); in particular \(g|_V\) is \(J\)-locally
1239	  injective. An isomorphism preserves local injectivity, hence \((F \triangleleft
1240	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
1241	  injectivity is local on the cover, \(F \triangleleft g\) is \(J\)-locally
1242	  injective. \emph{No stalks and no (d.2) stalk--tensor commutation enter}; the
1243	  single comparison lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (a presheaf-level
1244	  comparison, decomposed into its two open-immersion base-change halves) carries the
1245	  argument, and thereby moves \emph{onto} the critical path.
1246	
1247	  This is \emph{not} the section-level route that tensors a bare section map \(g(V)\)
1248	  --- only injective --- and stalls on a \(\mathrm{Tor}_1\)/flatness obligation. Here
1249	  the argument is sheaf-level and uses that \(g\) is locally \emph{bijective} (a
1250	  \(J.W\) morphism), reduced through the unitor to \(g|_V\) (again a \(J.W\)
1251	  morphism); a mere injection is never tensored, so no flatness is needed.
1252	
1253	  \emph{FALLBACK route (arbitrary \(F\); stalkwise, required only for the
1254	  full-generality route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path).} For an
1255	  \emph{arbitrary} (not necessarily locally trivial) \(F\), local injectivity is
1256	  proved stalkwise, by the argument Mathlib uses for fixed-base presheaves in
1257	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\)
1258	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
1259	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
1260	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
1261	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
1262	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
1263	  \[
1264	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
1265	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
1266	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
1267	  \]
1268	  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
1269	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
1270	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
1271	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).
1272	
1273	  The stalkwise computation rests on three ingredients with distinct status at the
1274	  pinned commit:

content:
=== how tensorObj_assoc_iso consumes whiskering (TensorObjSubstrate.lean L300-360) ===
located. The construction is the blueprint's three-step composite. Writing
`a = PresheafOfModules.sheafification (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)`,
`η = sheafificationAdjunction.unit` (the localizer unit, `toPresheaf`-image
`toSheafify ∈ J.W`), and `α` the presheaf-of-modules associator:
  1. `a(η_{M.val ⊗ᵖ N.val} ▷ P.val)` is iso  (P flat ⇒ right-whiskered `η ∈ J.W`
     by `W_whiskerRight_of_flat`; `a` inverts `J.W` by `isIso_sheafification_map_of_W`),
     giving `(M ⊗ N) ⊗ P = a(a(M.val⊗N.val).val ⊗ P.val) ≅ a((M.val⊗N.val) ⊗ P.val)`;
  2. `a.mapIso α : a((M.val⊗N.val)⊗P.val) ≅ a(M.val⊗(N.val⊗P.val))`;
  3. `a(M.val ◁ η_{N.val ⊗ᵖ P.val})` is iso  (M flat), giving
     `a(M.val⊗(N.val⊗P.val)) ≅ a(M.val ⊗ a(N.val⊗P.val).val) = M ⊗ (N ⊗ P)`.

**The go/no-go bridge `IsIso (a.map f)` from `J.W ((toPresheaf _).map f)` is now
CLOSED, axiom-clean**, as `PresheafOfModules.isIso_sheafification_map_of_W` (it is

  letI instMS : MonoidalCategoryStruct (_root_.PresheafOfModules (Sheaf.val X.ringCatSheaf)) :=
    inferInstanceAs (MonoidalCategoryStruct
      (_root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))

  set NP := PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj (R := X.presheaf) N.val P.val with hNP
  set η := (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction
    (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).unit with hη
  -- The two whiskered-unit localizer facts (ROUTE (d), via `W_whisker{Right,Left}_of_W`).

# blueprint-writer directive — chapter Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex (slug: d2wiring)

## Strategy context (the slice that matters)

(`lem:stalk_tensor_commutation`, `(A ⊗ᵖ B)_x ≅ A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x`) is now COMPLETE and

`TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean`). The whiskering lemma it contains is already blueprinted
in THIS chapter, so the file should be added to the coverage declaration.

## Required edits

### 1. Coverage declaration
Add `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/Vestigial.lean` to the `% archon:covers`
lines at the top of the chapter (one more `% archon:covers ...` line, matching existing style).

### 2. Expand the proof sketch of `lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk` (around line 2103–2141)

The current proof sketch correctly names the two-step idea (d.1: J.W ⟺ stalkwise iso; d.2:
identify the whiskered stalk with id ⊗ g_x) but is too thin for formalization. Rewrite the
`\begin{proof}` body to spell out THREE concrete movements, in the project's notation:

(a) **The d.1-bridge (site-level J.W ⟹ stalkwise isomorphism on `Opens X`).** A morphism
`g` of presheaves of modules over the topological site `Opens X` with `(toPresheaf).map g ∈ J.W`
has, at every point `x`, an isomorphism of `Ab`-stalks `(stalkFunctor Ab x).map ((toPresheaf).map g)`.
The Mathlib ingredients are: `CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.W.isLocallyInjective`
(J.W → locally injective) and the corresponding local surjectivity, together with the TopCat
stalk criteria `TopCat.Presheaf.app_injective_iff_stalkFunctor_map_injective` and the
locally-surjective-on-stalks characterisation; equivalently, on the topological site one may use
`TopCat.Presheaf.isIso_iff_stalkFunctor_map_iso` after identifying local bijectivity with a
sheaf-isomorphism on the associated sheaves. State this as a NAMED sub-lemma so it can be pinned:

  \begin{lemma}[d.1-bridge: locally bijective implies stalkwise iso]
    \label{lem:W_implies_stalkwise_iso}
    \lean{PresheafOfModules.isIso_stalkFunctor_map_of_W}   % suggested Lean name; prover may adjust
    \uses{lem:stalk_linear_map}
    For a morphism g of presheaves of R-modules over the small site Opens X with
    (toPresheaf).map g ∈ J.W, the induced Ab-stalk map at every x is an isomorphism.
  \end{lemma}
with a short proof sketch citing the Mathlib lemmas above.

(b) **B-argument naturality of `stalkTensorIso` (NEW helper, the genuinely new ingredient).**
`stalkTensorIso` is constructed for fixed A, B. To identify the whiskered stalk we need that it
is natural in the SECOND tensor factor: for g : M → N, the square relating
`(A ⊗ᵖ g)_x` (the stalk of the whiskered map) to `LinearMap.lTensor A_x (stalkLinearMap g x)`
commutes, i.e. `stalkTensorIso A N ∘ (id_A ⊗ g)_x = lTensor A_x g_x ∘ stalkTensorIso A M`.
State this as a NAMED sub-lemma:

  \begin{lemma}[B-naturality of the stalk–tensor comparison]
    \label{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation_naturality_right}
    \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorIso_naturality_right}   % suggested; prover may adjust
    \uses{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation, lem:stalk_linear_map}
    For g : M → N, under stalkTensorIso the stalk of A ◁ g is identified with
    LinearMap.lTensor A_x (stalkLinearMap g x).
  \end{lemma}
Proof sketch: both sides are determined on germ generators germ(a) ⊗ germ(m) by the germ
characterisations `stalkTensorLinearMap_germ_tmul` / `stalkTensorRev_germ_tmul` and
`stalkLinearMap_germ`; check the identity there and extend by `TensorProduct.induction_on`.

(c) **Conclude.** Since each `g_x` is an iso (by (a)) and `stalkLinearMap g x` is then a linear
equivalence (`stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso`, already built), `LinearMap.lTensor A_x (g_x)` is an iso
(tensoring an equivalence by the identity — `LinearEquiv.lTensor`, flatness-free). By (b),
`(A ◁ g)_x` is therefore an iso for every x; by the d.1-bridge (a) in reverse, `A ◁ g ∈ J.W`,
hence is J-locally injective (`GrothendieckTopology.W.isLocallyInjective`). No flatness, no local
triviality; F (= A) arbitrary.

Add a short note: the Lean lemma is stated over a general site J but is only ever consumed at
`R := X.presheaf` (the topological site of a scheme); the proof specialises to `Opens X` where
stalks exist. The declaration is UNPROTECTED, so the prover may re-sign it to the topological
site if needed; its only consumers `W_whiskerLeft_of_W` / `W_whiskerRight_of_W` and ultimately
`tensorObj_assoc_iso` already instantiate at `X.presheaf`.

### 3. (Optional, if natural) note the consumer repoint

repoints it to `thm:pic_commgroup` once the carrier group lands. You need not change it now;
just leave the existing standing note intact.

you want to cite the stalkwise-localizer principle, Stacks tag 01CR / the conservative-family
